rubric_en: When Ethel and Julius Rosenberg were executed by the U.S. government, they left behind two sons, ages 6 and 10. All these years later, Robby and Michael are still trying to make sense of what happened.
rubric_zh: 当尤塞尔和朱利叶斯·罗森伯格被美国政府处决时，他们留下了两个年幼的儿子，分别是6岁和10岁。多年以后，罗比和迈克尔仍在努力理解当年的发生。

paragraphs.en: Robby and Michael Rosenberg went to the White House with a rabbi and their grandmother in June 1953 to ask President Eisenhower to spare their parents’ lives. (Hank Walker / LIFE Picture Collection / Shutterstock)
paragraphs.zh: 1953年6月，罗比和迈克尔·罗森伯格与一位拉比以及祖母一起去了白宫，向艾森豪威尔总统请求饶恕他们父母的性命。（Hank Walker / LIFE图片集 / Shutterstock）

paragraphs.en: For those old enough to remember, Michael and Robby Meeropol will always be the Rosenberg boys.
paragraphs.zh: 对于那些年岁够大、还记得当年的读者来说，迈克尔和罗比·米罗波尔始终都会被称作“罗森伯格兄弟”。

paragraphs.en: I never knew them as such, but it’s not hard to imagine what they were like, in part because there are so many pictures. In one, from June 1953, they are sitting outside the White House in shirts and ties, wool coats, and Brooklyn Dodgers caps. Six-year-old Robby holds his grandmother’s hand; to his right stands a rabbi.
paragraphs.zh: 我从未真正认识他们，但我们并不难想象他们的样子，部分原因是照片太多了。其中一张是1953年6月拍的，照片里他们穿着衬衫和领带、羊毛外套和布鲁克林道奇帽，在白宫外坐着。6岁的罗比握着祖母的手；他的右侧站着一位拉比。

paragraphs.en: The boys look uncomfortable, and solemn. Their parents, Ethel and Julius, have been convicted of spying for the Soviet Union and sentenced to death. Robby and Michael are there to ask President Dwight Eisenhower to spare their lives. Michael, who is 10, has written a letter that he will hand to a White House guard: “Please let my mommy and daddy go and not let anything happen to them. If they come home Robby and I will be very happy we will thank you very much.”
paragraphs.zh: 这两个男孩看上去不自在而且严肃。因为他们的父母尤塞尔和朱利叶斯被判定为替苏联间谍活动并被判处死刑。罗比和迈克尔来到这里请求德怀特·艾森豪威尔总统饶他们一命。10岁的迈克尔写了一封信，准备交给白宫的卫兵：“请让我的爸爸妈妈回家，不要让任何事发生在他们身上。如果他们回家，罗比和我都会非常开心，我们会非常感谢您的帮助。”

paragraphs.en: Out of the frame, but documented in other photos from that day, are the placards that protesters carried invoking Robby and Michael, the helpless boys made symbols of what many believed was a grave injustice about to be perpetrated against their parents. (Counterprotesters, carrying signs saying FRY ’EM and HANG ’EM, disagreed.) Similar scenes played out around the world. After Pope Pius XII called for clemency, the Vatican’s newspaper cited the “two little innocents on whose soul and destiny the death of their parents would forever leave sinister scars.”
paragraphs.zh: 在照片框外，但在那一天的其他照片中有记载的是，抗议者举着标语牌，提到罗比和迈克尔，这两个无助的孩子被视为许多人认为即将对其父母实施的一次重大不公的象征。（反对者举着写着“FRY ’EM”和“HANG ’EM”的牌子，表示不同意见。）相似的场景在世界各地上演。教宗庇护十二世呼吁施以宽恕后，梵蒂冈的报纸引用了“那两个小无辜者，他们的父母去世后，在他们的灵魂与命运上将永远留下阴影般的伤痕。”

paragraphs.en: I learned the broad outlines of the events that followed—the electric chair, and the orphans it left behind—half a century later, on a weekend visit to the home of my parents’ friends Robby and Elli in western Massachusetts. I was 8. I don’t recall how the subject came up or exactly what I was told, but I remember trying to picture the electrocution, and that I couldn’t sleep that night. I knew Robby mostly as the kind of adult who enjoys making bad puns and funny faces for kids’ amusement. The notion that, as a kid himself, he had been orphaned by the United States government didn’t seem to match his playful persona. I wondered, but didn’t quite know how to ask, how anyone could go on living after something like that had happened. And how could he possibly seem so normal?
paragraphs.zh: 我对此后发生事件的基本轮廓——电椅和由此留下的一群孤儿——是在五十多年后、一次周末前往我父母的朋友罗比和埃莉在马萨诸塞州西部的家中才得知的。我那时8岁。我不记得当时这个话题是怎么被提起的，也不清楚我被告知了什么细节，但我记得自己试着去想象电刑那一幕，而且那一晚我没法入睡。我最初更多把罗比当作那种喜欢讲冷笑话、给孩子们做滑稽表情来取乐的大人。想到他自己也曾是个孩子，却因美国政府而成了孤儿，这和他那种嬉皮玩世不恭的形象完全不匹配。我心里很困惑，但又说不出该如何提问：发生这样的事后，人生怎么可能还继续下去？他又怎么可能看起来还这么正常？

paragraphs.en: I later learned more: that Robby and Michael had been adopted after the executions, taken a new last name, and all but disappeared from the public eye. They went to college and graduate school, got married, had children of their own—only to reemerge as public figures in the 1970s, vowing to clear their parents’ names. From then on, they’d never really stopped talking about Ethel and Julius, or pressing the government for answers about its evidence and motivations for seeking their death. By the time I was old enough to observe their advocacy firsthand—at a public event commemorating the executions’ 50th anniversary, and later in a presentation Robby gave at my high school—I understood their basic posture to be one of resolute protest. Ethel and Julius, they maintained, had not been atomic spies. They had been scapegoated for their political allegiances, framed by prosecutors, and wrongfully killed.
paragraphs.zh: 我后来了解得更多：罗比和迈克尔在处决后被领养，改了新姓氏，并且几乎从公众视野中消失。后来他们上了大学、上了研究生院，结婚生子——直到1970年代，他们又重新出现在公共视野中，誓言为父母洗清冤名。从那时起，他们几乎从未停止过谈论伊特尔和朱利叶斯，或向政府追问其定罪证据和要求其死刑动机。等到我长大并亲眼见到他们的倡议行动时——在一场纪念处决50周年的公共活动上，以及后来罗比在我高中的一次演讲中——我明白了他们的基本立场始终是坚定抗议：伊特尔和朱利叶斯并不是原子间谍。他们是因为政治立场而被当作替罪羊、被检方构陷，并被不公正地处决。

paragraphs.en: This was not, I came to understand, an uncontroversial position. One year, my family’s Yom Kippur break-fast meal was very nearly derailed when my dad got into an argument with one of our guests about the Rosenbergs. Julius and Ethel, this guest insisted, were both guilty as charged, and to pretend otherwise was a pinko fantasy. The incident left me with yet more questions about my parents’ friend and the complicated historical legacy he’d inherited.
paragraphs.zh: 我后来才明白，这并不是一个没有争议的立场。有一年，在我家的赎罪日封斋开斋晚餐上，我爸与一位来客几乎闹得不欢而散，原因就是罗森伯格事件。那位客人坚持认为朱利叶斯和伊特尔都“罪有应得”，而假装他们无辜只是“老左派”式的幻想。那次争执让我对父母那位朋友产生了更多疑问，也更困惑于他所承继的那段复杂历史遗产。

paragraphs.en: I’ve lately been able to ask Robby and Michael many of these questions directly—and to go beyond the abbreviated, textbook version of their family’s story, deep into the countless primary and secondary sources that, for nearly 75 years, have helped shape an ever-evolving debate about what, in this case, constitutes truth, and what justice really means. The facts are messier and more resistant to facile conclusions than either side of the argument might care to acknowledge. Even now, new ones continue to emerge.
paragraphs.zh: 最近我终于能够直接向罗比和迈克尔提出许多这些问题，并且深入到他们家族故事的标准教科书版之外，去查阅过去近75年来塑造这场不断演变争论的无数一手与二手资料：在这场争论中，什么才算真相、正义究竟意味着什么。事实比任何一方都愿意承认的要复杂得多，也更难得出浅显的结论。即便在今天，新的资料仍在不断出现。

paragraphs.en: Michael ( left ) and Robby with items from the Rosenberg papers at Boston University, more than 70 years after the executions (Christopher Churchill for The Atlantic; Julius and Ethel Rosenberg collection at the Howard Gotlieb Archival Research Center, Boston University Libraries)
paragraphs.zh: 迈克尔（左）和罗比在波士顿大学查看罗森伯格案资料，距处决已经过去了70多年（来源：克里斯托弗·丘吉尔摄影，刊登于《大西洋月刊》；朱利叶斯与伊瑟尔·罗森伯格资料藏于波士顿大学图书馆霍华德·戈德莱布档案研究中心）

paragraphs.en: Since the fall of the Soviet Union 35 years ago, government files have been declassified and grand-jury testimonies unsealed; key players have made dramatic confessions. Robby and Michael, the old men who were once such young boys, have had to make their own sense of these revelations. Not all of them have been easy to accept.
paragraphs.zh: 自35年前苏联解体以来，政府档案被解密，大陪审团证词也被解封；关键人物都做出了惊人的供认。罗比和迈克尔——曾经还是年幼的男孩，如今的老人——不得不亲自消化这些新发现。并非所有发现都容易接受。

paragraphs.en: In April of last year, I spent a day with the brothers in Cold Spring, New York, where Michael and his late wife, Ann, moved in 2009. The town sits on a hill overlooking the Hudson River, and I got off the train from New York City that morning with hikers who fanned out to the nearby trails.
paragraphs.zh: 去年4月，我在纽约州冷泉镇（Cold Spring）与这对兄弟共度了一天。迈克尔和已故妻子安妮于2009年搬到这里。这个小镇坐落在一座俯瞰哈德逊河的山坡上，那天清晨我从纽约市乘火车抵达，与一群徒步者下车后，他们便分道扬镳去附近的步道。

paragraphs.en: Robby was waiting to pick me up at the station in his red Toyota; he’d arrived early, as he is apt to do. On the short drive to Michael’s condo, we made easy small talk. I’d made clear that the article I was writing would be solely mine to shape, and they’d agreed that I could ask them anything. But I knew that once I turned my recorder on, I’d need to ask questions that might put me in league, in their minds, with the legions of other journalists they believed had fixated on the wrong angles, or missed the point of the Rosenberg case entirely.
paragraphs.zh: 罗比在车站等我。他开着他的红色丰田车来接我，像往常一样提前到达了。我们在去迈克尔公寓的短途车程中聊着简单的闲话。我很清楚自己写这篇文章时有完全的编辑自主权，我们也达成了共识——我可以问他们任何问题。但我知道，一旦我把录音机打开，就得提问那些可能在他们看来把我拉进与一群其他记者同样的行列的问题；那些记者要么只盯着错误的角度，要么完全没有抓住罗森伯格案的要点。

paragraphs.en: Before I started reporting this story, I’d met Michael only in passing. We’d now talked on the phone a few times, and my main takeaway was that his being a retired economics professor made a lot of sense. His tendency was to carefully (and loquaciously) argue a point, and he had limitless enthusiasm for wading into the minutiae of legal details and chronology. Robby, by his own admission, preferred to focus on more general themes. As the three of us sat talking in Michael’s living room, Robby pointed out that his brother, being four years older, had spent far more time with their father than he had. Robby seemed to think of Julius more as a historical figure he happened to be related to, while Michael, on some level, still mourned the father he’d lost as a 10-year-old.
paragraphs.zh: 在开始报道这个故事之前，我只在路上匆匆见过迈克尔几次。后来我们又通了几次电话，我的第一印象是，他是一位退休的经济学教授这一点非常说得通。他的谈吐方式是先谨慎地、并且滔滔不绝地把一个观点讲完整；他对深入讨论法律细节和时间线中的细枝末节也有无穷无尽的热情。罗比则公开承认，他更愿意聚焦更宏观的主题。当我们三个人坐在迈克尔客厅里聊天时，罗比指出，他的哥哥比他大四岁，因此和父亲在一起的时间更多。罗比似乎把朱利叶斯更多地当作一位历史人物来想，和自己有血缘关系；而迈克尔则在某种程度上，仍在悼念那个在他10岁时就离世的父亲。

paragraphs.en: Julius was a co-owner of a struggling machine shop on the Lower East Side. On weekends, he would take Michael to ride the New York City subways and elevated trains for hours on end; they would stand at the very front, where they could look out at the switching tracks. Ethel stayed home with Robby. She worked hard at being a good mother—she sang to her boys, read Parents’ magazine, took a class in child psychology. In their small apartment near the Manhattan Bridge, the kids shared the lone bedroom while their parents slept in the living room.
paragraphs.zh: 朱利叶斯是曼哈顿下城一家困难的金属机修厂的共同经营者。周末时，他会带迈克尔坐纽约地铁和高架列车几个小时；他们总是站在最前面的位置，靠近可以看到道岔的地方。伊瑟尔则和罗比在家。她努力做好母亲：会给儿子们唱歌、看《父母》杂志，还上过一门儿童心理学课程。在他们住在曼哈顿大桥附近的小公寓里，孩子们共享唯一的卧室，父母则睡在客厅里。

paragraphs.en: Listening to Michael’s descriptions, you could almost forget that his early years were different from those of other postwar American kids: “I’d have a wooden baseball bat and a Spalding ball or a tennis ball. He”—Julius—“would pitch it to me, and I would hit it.” Michael was listening to The Lone Ranger on the radio when, one July night in 1950, FBI agents came to the door to arrest his father.
paragraphs.zh: 听迈克尔这么讲，几乎会忘记他早年的经历与其他战后美国孩子不同：‘我会有一支木制棒球棒，一颗斯伯丁球，或者一颗网球。是他——朱利叶斯——把球抛来，我去击球。’1950年一个七月的夜晚，迈克尔正在听广播剧《孤星枪手》，这时联邦调查局特工敲门来抓他的父亲。

paragraphs.en: In his letters from jail and later prison, Julius tried to reassure his sons. “Don’t you worry,” he wrote; “we’ll be playing games again as soon as I can straighten out this trouble I’m in.” But on August 11, after Ethel appeared before the grand jury that had indicted Julius and refused to answer any questions, she, too, was arrested. Neither of them ever came home again.
paragraphs.zh: 在狱中的信里以及后来在监狱里，朱利叶斯不断试图安慰儿子们。“别担心，”他写道，“一旦我把这场麻烦解决好，我们就会马上再玩游戏。”但在8月11日，当伊瑟尔在起诉朱利叶斯的特别大陪审团前拒绝回答任何问题后，也被捕了。此后，他们中的任何一个都再也没有回到家里。

paragraphs.en: After his mother’s arrest, Michael told an aunt that he wanted to run into the street and get killed by a car. He stopped eating. He drew endless railroad tracks, switching and merging and looping but going nowhere. When he complained to his grandmother Tessie Greenglass, with whom he and Robby were staying, she told him to send Ethel a telegram. But he didn’t want to upset his mother, so he said that he was “having a nice time. I miss you very much. Love, your son, Michael.”
paragraphs.zh: 在母亲被捕后，迈克尔告诉一位姑姑说他想冲到马路上，被汽车撞死。他不再吃饭。他不断画着无尽的铁路轨道，岔到这边又并到那边，循环往复，却始终走向不明。后来他向祖母特西·格林格拉斯抱怨，她与罗比一起住在那里。她建议他给伊瑟尔发一张电报。但他不想让母亲担心，于是他写道：“我过得很好。我非常想念你。爱你的儿子，迈克尔。”

paragraphs.en: The boys were eventually sent to the Hebrew Children’s Home in the Bronx, where attendants beat and ridiculed children. To Robby, it felt as if he and Michael were imprisoned, just like Ethel and Julius. He didn’t understand what his parents had been accused of doing. Instead, he harbored an anxious sense that some menacing force had broken up his family—and stood ready to do more damage if given an opening. He would try to avoid attracting attention, lest “they” take notice and wreak more havoc. He also developed a coping mechanism that I sensed had become a lifelong habit: “Whenever something was really bad,” he told me, “I always figured out some way to say, Oh, it’s no big deal. It’s not happening. It’s a form of denial, I guess.”
paragraphs.zh: 这两个男孩最终被送进布朗克斯的“希伯来儿童之家”，在那里护工会殴打和嘲笑孩子们。对罗比来说，这让他和迈克尔感觉自己像被囚禁了一样，就像伊瑟尔和朱利叶斯一样。他不明白父母究竟被指控了什么。相反，他心里一直有一种焦虑感：某种带有威胁性的力量已经把他的家庭打散——而且只要有机会就会继续伤害。他会尽量避免引人注意，生怕“他们”注意到他，从而再制造一场灾难。他还形成了一个应对方式，我察觉这已成了他一生的习惯：“每当事情真的很糟，我总能想办法告诉自己，哦，不算什么。这根本没发生。大概这是一种否认吧。”

paragraphs.en: As I watched the brothers that day in Cold Spring, they struck me as, above all, brothers—by turns affectionate and peeved with one another, as likely to use a childhood nickname as to sigh in exasperation. They are fluent in the vigorous conversational cadence of older Jewish men raised in mid-century New York. “Verbal combat,” Robby later said, was a favored family pastime. But neither of them seemed eager to undergo much emotional probing.
paragraphs.zh: 那天在冷泉，我看见这对兄弟时，首先映入眼帘的是——他们当然是兄弟——一会儿亲昵，一会儿又生气，像在童年玩笑里互称外号，又像在烦躁中叹气。两人都精通那种20世纪中期纽约犹太老一辈特有的有力对话节奏。罗比后来说，“口头交锋”是家里最喜欢的一种消遣活动。但他们两个都似乎并不热衷于做太多情绪上的深究。

paragraphs.en: At one point that afternoon, Michael spent a few minutes recounting the songs his parents had played for them as kids, enjoying himself as he quoted what Julius had called “nonsense” lyrics from “Oh! Susanna” and other “camp songs.” If not quite fully transported back in time, he appeared willing, at least, to try going there.
paragraphs.zh: 那天下午的某个时刻，迈克尔花了几分钟回忆起父母在他们小时候为他们唱过的歌，并在引用朱利叶斯称为“荒谬”的《Oh! Susanna》歌词和其他“营地歌曲”时乐在其中。即便没有完全穿越回过去，他起码看起来愿意至少尝试走进那段时光。

paragraphs.en: Without meaning to, I broke the spell. As Michael began to reflect on his relationship with Ethel, and his sense that he’d been a “bad boy,” frequently misbehaving, I tried to prompt Robby to add his own recollections from that pre-arrest period. “I don’t know,” Robby said, and paused. Maybe it was too much to expect a 3-year-old to have formed clear memories. In any case, he quickly changed the subject back to their decades of advocacy work.
paragraphs.zh: 不经意间，我打断了这份氛围。迈克尔开始回忆自己与伊瑟尔的关系，以及他总觉得自己曾是个“坏男孩”、经常捣蛋时，我便试图引导罗比补充他们被捕前那段时期的个人记忆。“我不知道。”罗比说着顿了一下。也许对一个3岁的孩子来说，要求他有清晰的记忆本来就过于苛刻。无论如何，他很快又把话题转回到他们几十年的倡导工作上。

paragraphs.en: Before I boarded my return train to New York City, Michael reminded me to look out the window on my left. A few stops away, in Ossining, the barbed-wire fences and high turrets of Sing Sing towered over the tracks. In opting to spend his retirement near his grandkids, Michael had ended up living half an hour from the site of his parents’ deaths.
paragraphs.zh: 在我乘坐返程列车回纽约市之前，迈克尔提醒我留意列车左侧的窗外。几站之外，在奥辛宁，辛辛监狱那带刺铁丝网的围栏和高高的哨塔耸立在铁轨之上。迈克尔选择在离孙辈更近的地方度过退休生活，结果最终住在离父母被处决地点仅半小时车程的地方。

paragraphs.en: Robby and Michael visited their parents in prison for the first time in the summer of 1951, after more than a year apart. By then, the boys had gotten used to the Hebrew Children’s Home, and then they had gotten picked up from it. Now they were staying with their “Bubbie,” Sophie Rosenberg, who played with them and cooked foods they liked. Still, they were eager to see their parents again.
paragraphs.zh: 罗比和迈克尔在1951年夏天时第一次探望了入狱的父母，那时两人已经分离一年多。到那时，孩子们已经习惯了希伯来儿童之家，之后又从那里被领走。现在他们与“祖母”索菲·罗森伯格住在一起，她会和他们玩耍，还会给他们做喜欢的食物。尽管如此，他们仍然渴望再次见到父母。

paragraphs.en: Julius and Ethel were separated in prison, allowed to see each other only once a week or so. Mostly, they communicated through letters. Anxious to put the boys at ease, Julius wrote to Ethel that he planned to draw them pictures of trains, boats, and buses. “Hah! You can’t make me jealous with your boats and trains,” she replied. “I have an envelope full of rare specimens collected with painstaking care by that intrepid hunter of wild insects, namely, your wife!” She worried that Robby in particular “may be a little shy and strange with us.”
paragraphs.zh: 朱利叶斯和埃塞尔在监狱里被分开关押，他们大约每周只能见面一次。大多数时候，他们主要靠写信交流。为了让孩子们安心，朱利叶斯写信告诉埃塞尔说他打算给他们画火车、船和公共汽车的图画。“哈，你拿着你的船和火车可叫不出我的嫉妒，”她回信说。“我有一个信封，里面装着许多珍贵标本，是那位勇敢的野生昆虫猎手——也就是你妻子——费心采集的。” 她担心罗比“对我们可能有些害羞和陌生”。

paragraphs.en: In another letter to Julius, Ethel imagined how they might explain their situation to the boys. “Of course, we feel badly that we are separated from you but we also know that we are not guilty and that an injustice has been done to us by people who solved their own problems by lying about us,” she wrote. “It’s all right to feel any way you like about them, so long as your feelings don’t give you pain and make you unhappy.”
paragraphs.zh: 在另一封写给朱利叶斯的信里，埃塞尔想象她们如何向孩子们解释当下的处境。“当然，我们为与您分开而难过，但我们也知道自己是无辜的，而且有人为了替自己脱罪，撒谎诋毁我们，这是对我们的冤枉。”她写道。“你可以对他们有任何情绪，只要这些情绪不会让你痛苦，也不会让你不快乐。”

paragraphs.en: At Sing Sing, the boys saw Ethel first, then Julius. Determined to prove his fearlessness, Michael asked to see the electric chair for himself. Ethel had suggested that Julius describe its effect as “painless electrocution,” similar to “a highly magnified electric shock that anybody might sustain.”
paragraphs.zh: 在辛辛监狱，孩子们先见到了埃塞尔，随后见到了朱利叶斯。为了证明自己不害怕，迈克尔提出要亲自去看电椅。埃塞尔曾建议朱利叶斯把它的效果描述为“无痛电击”，与“任何人都可能承受的极度放大的电击”相似。

paragraphs.en: “The fact is both children are disturbed,” Julius wrote after the visit.
paragraphs.zh: “事实上，两个孩子都很受惊，”朱利叶斯在探视结束后写道。

paragraphs.en: The executions had been set for May 1951, but the date kept getting pushed back as appeals made their way through the legal system. On subsequent visits, all four Rosenbergs were allowed to be together, and the mood of their reunions became lighter. As a family, they laughed and sang songs; sometimes, they played hangman. When Michael asked his parents if they were truly innocent, they assured him that they were.
paragraphs.zh: 执行日期 originally 被定在1951年5月，但随着上诉案件在司法系统中推进，日期一再被推迟。之后的探视中，四个罗森堡一家都被允许在一起，他们的团聚气氛也逐渐轻松起来。作为一个家庭，他们会笑、会一起唱歌；有时还会玩猜词游戏（Hangman）。当迈克尔问父母是否真的无辜时，他们向他保证自己是无辜的。

paragraphs.en: “You boys are our greatest pleasure and joy in life and we love you more than anything else in the world,” Julius wrote in one letter. And indeed, it’s hard to read the letters, and the various accounts of these visits, and come to any conclusion other than that Ethel and Julius loved their sons. They agonized over the details of the boys’ care and cursed the government for separating them.
paragraphs.zh: “你们俩是我们生命中最大的快乐和幸福，我们爱你们胜过世界上任何事。”朱利叶斯在一封信中写道。确实，读这些信件，以及这些探视的各种记载，很难得出除了伊瑟尔和朱利叶斯深爱他们的儿子之外的其他结论。

paragraphs.en: But it’s also hard not to question why they didn’t do more to prevent their sons from being orphaned—why they chose not to take advantage of the numerous opportunities they were given to cooperate with the authorities, and to live.
paragraphs.zh: 但同样很难不去追问，为什么他们没有做更多事情来避免两个儿子成为孤儿——为什么他们没有选择不放过一个个机会，与当局合作并继续活下去。

paragraphs.en: Ethel’s younger brother David Greenglass made a different choice. When Klaus Fuchs was arrested by the British in early 1950 for passing atomic plans to the Soviets, his testimony led American authorities to Harry Gold, a chemist. Gold in turn led them to David, who had been a machinist at Los Alamos during World War II, and his wife, Ruth.
paragraphs.zh: 伊瑟尔的弟弟大卫·格林格拉斯做了不同的选择。1950年初，克劳斯·富克斯在英国被捕后，被指控向苏联传递原子计划，他的证词把美国当局引向了化学家哈里·戈德。戈德又把他们带到了大卫身上。大卫在二战期间曾在洛斯阿拉莫斯担任机械师，并引出了他的妻子露丝。

paragraphs.en: The Greenglasses quickly confessed that they had been part of a spy ring led by Julius Rosenberg, through whom they said they had shared sketches of the atomic bomb’s design with the Soviets in 1945. Ruth said that Julius had told her to recruit David, and that Ethel was there and had been encouraging.
paragraphs.zh: 格林格拉斯夫妇很快供认，他们曾是由朱利叶斯·罗森伯格领导的一个间谍组织成员，并说他们通过他在1945年将原子弹设计图纸的草图提供给了苏联。鲁思说朱利叶斯曾告诉她要招募戴维，且伊瑟尔也在场，并且一直在起鼓励作用。

paragraphs.en: The FBI was certain that Julius had more valuable information, and hoped that he would confess and name names, just as his brother- and sister-in-law had. But Julius denied everything, and refused to point a finger at anyone else. So J. Edgar Hoover decided to adopt a recommendation from a subordinate, who wrote that agents should “consider every possible means” to make Julius talk—including bringing charges against Ethel. Hoover wrote to the attorney general: “Proceeding against his wife might serve as a lever.”
paragraphs.zh: 联邦调查局确信朱利叶斯掌握了更有价值的信息，并希望他像他的姻亲和姐夫那样认罪并指认他人。然而朱利叶斯对所有指控都予以否认，并拒绝点名其他人。于是，J·爱德加·胡佛决定采纳一名下属的建议，该下属写道，特工应“动用一切可能的手段”来逼迫朱利叶斯开口，包括对伊瑟尔提出指控。胡佛在给司法部长的信中写道：“起诉他的妻子可能会起到杠杆作用。”

paragraphs.en: The Greenglasses’ testimony gave the government enough evidence for an arrest warrant. But like Julius, Ethel told the authorities nothing—nor did her arrest have the intended effect on her husband. The trial, though, brought out new testimony from the Greenglasses, who said that Ethel hadn’t just been a supportive bystander, but had typed up David’s hard-to-read notes on classified atomic science. Now the death penalty seemed like a real possibility for both Rosenbergs.
paragraphs.zh: 格林格拉斯夫妇的证词已经为政府提供了足够的证据来申请逮捕令。但与朱利叶斯一样，伊瑟尔对当局一句实情也不吐露——她的逮捕也没有产生对丈夫预期中的影响。审判中，格林格拉斯夫妇又提供了新的证词，他们称伊瑟尔不仅仅是“支持性的旁观者”，而且还代替戴维把难以辨认的机密核科学笔记打了字稿。此时，罗森伯格夫妇都被认为有可能面临死刑。

paragraphs.en: At the sentencing, Judge Irving R. Kaufman said that he considered the couple’s actions “worse than murder”—a crime with untold numbers of past and future victims. Russian access to the atomic bomb, Kaufman said, had “already caused, in my opinion, the Communist aggression in Korea, with the resultant casualties exceeding 50,000 and who knows but that millions more of innocent people may pay the price of your treason.” Kaufman made clear that both husband and wife were to blame. “Julius Rosenberg was the prime mover in this conspiracy,” he said, but Ethel was a “full-fledged partner.” Both therefore deserved to die.
paragraphs.zh: 在宣判时，法官欧文·R·考夫曼表示，他认为这对夫妇的行为“比谋杀更恶劣”——那是一种会造成无数过去和未来受害者的罪行。考夫曼说，苏联人获取原子弹“已经在我看来导致了朝鲜战争中的共产主义侵略”，并造成了超过5万人的伤亡，而“谁能说得准，不会有更多数以百万计的无辜者为你们的叛国行为付出代价。”考夫曼明确表示，这对丈夫和妻子都要负责。‘朱利叶斯·罗森伯格是这起阴谋的策划主导者’，他这样说道，但伊瑟尔是‘完全参与的同谋’。因此，他们都应被处以死刑。

paragraphs.en: Subsequent revelations from multiple prosecutors in the case have shown that the judge didn’t come to this conclusion entirely on his own. Roy Cohn, who was an assistant U.S. attorney on the case, later said that prosecutors had had clandestine phone conversations with Kaufman, “especially about whether Ethel should be sentenced to death.”
paragraphs.zh: 该案后续中，多位检察官公开表示，法官并非完全凭自己决定这一结论。后来，检察官助理罗伊·科恩说，检察官团队曾与考夫曼进行过秘密电话沟通，“尤其是关于是否应判伊瑟尔死刑”这一问题。

paragraphs.en: The Rosenbergs’ co-defendant, Morton Sobell, a former classmate of Julius’s at City College, also claimed total innocence. Sobell was convicted of conspiracy to commit espionage and sentenced to 30 years in prison (he was spared the death penalty because no evidence tied him to sharing atomic secrets). David was sentenced to 15 years in prison and served less than 10. Ruth, who was never charged, stayed with their two young children.
paragraphs.zh: 罗森堡的共同被告、朱利叶斯在城市学院的前同学莫顿·索贝尔也声称自己完全无罪。索贝尔被判阴谋从事间谍活动罪成立，判处30年监禁（他被免于死刑，因为没有证据表明他参与了核机密的泄露）。大卫被判15年监禁，实际服刑不足10年。鲁思从未被起诉，她与两个年幼的孩子一起生活。

paragraphs.en: A few weeks before their deaths, the Rosenbergs were visited at Sing Sing by James V. Bennett, the director of the Bureau of Prisons. Bennett said he’d been sent by the attorney general with an offer: If they were ready to talk, officials would recommend clemency.
paragraphs.zh: 在他们被处决前几周，罗森堡夫妇在辛辛监狱会见了监狱局局长詹姆斯·V·贝内特。贝内特表示，司法部长派他前来传话：如果他们愿意开口配合，官方将建议对他们执行大赦。

paragraphs.en: Ethel was outraged. “I made it short and sweet,” she wrote to her lawyer. “I was innocent, my husband was innocent, and neither of us knew anything about espionage.” What right did the government have “to try to forcibly wring from us a false confession, by dangling our lives before us like bait before hapless fish! Pay the price we demand, or forfeit your lives, is that the idea?” Bennett pleaded with her to change her mind.
paragraphs.zh: 伊瑟尔十分愤怒。她在写给律师的信中说：“我写得很简短、很直接。”“我清白，我丈夫清白，我们俩对间谍活动一无所知。”政府有什么权利“把我们吊在一起逼我们交代，拿我们的生命当成鱼饵吊在我们面前，像笨鱼一样？”“要么按我们所要的付出代价，要么放弃你们的生命——这就是你们的逻辑吗？”贝内特恳请她改变想法。

paragraphs.en: The deputy attorney general later said of Ethel, “She called our bluff.”
paragraphs.zh: 副司法部长后来谈到埃塞尔时说：“她识破了我们这次虚张声势的把戏。”

paragraphs.en: In June 1953, the day after their 14th wedding anniversary, after several last-minute appeals—including to the U.S. Supreme Court—had failed, Julius and Ethel Rosenberg, ages 35 and 37, had one final afternoon together, separated by wire mesh. Ethel wrote a letter to their sons that they would both sign: “We wish we might have had the tremendous joy and gratification of living our lives out with you,” it said. “Always remember that we were innocent and could not wrong our conscience. We press you close and kiss you with all our strength.”
paragraphs.zh: 1953年6月，在结婚14周年纪念日之后的第二天，在数次临时上诉——包括向美国最高法院的上诉——全部失败后，35岁的朱利叶斯和37岁的埃塞尔·罗森伯格只剩下一次短暂的黄昏，彼此却只能通过铁丝网隔开。埃塞尔写下了一封信给他们的两个儿子，并由两人共同签名：信中写道："我们真希望能与你们一起度过人生，拥有这份无比的喜悦和满足感。" "请永远记住，我们是清白的，决不会违背自己的良心。我们把你们紧紧抱在心里，用尽全部力量给你们一个吻。"

paragraphs.en: The letter would have a much wider audience than just Robby and Michael, who were by then living with family friends in New Jersey. The uncertain fate of the Rosenbergs had become global news, and the couple’s letters were being distributed in dozens of countries via pamphlets and newspapers. From December 1952 to June 1953, 80 rallies in support of the Rosenbergs were held in Paris alone. One French diplomat told Secretary of State John Foster Dulles that the case was “the most troublesome issue affecting relations between the United States and Europe.”
paragraphs.zh: 这封信将比罗比和迈克尔更广泛地被阅读。彼时，两兄弟已经与新泽西州的一对家庭朋友住在一起。罗森伯格夫妇命运的悬而未决成为了全球新闻，他们的书信通过传单和报纸在几十个国家流传。仅从1952年12月到1953年6月，巴黎就举行了80场支持罗森伯格的集会。一位法国外交官曾对国务卿约翰·福斯特·杜勒斯说，这一案子是“影响美欧关系的最棘手问题”。

paragraphs.en: “PLEASE don’t let my parents DIE. My little Brother and I need and miss them,” Michael wrote in a letter to a left-wing weekly. (Christopher Churchill for The Atlantic)
paragraphs.zh: “请不要让我的父母去世。我的小弟弟和我需要他们，也想念他们。”迈克尔在写给一家左翼周刊的信中这样说。（克里斯托弗·丘吉尔，载于《大西洋月刊》）

paragraphs.en: Last summer, Robby, Michael, and I visited the archives at Boston University’s library, where many of their parents’ prison letters are housed. As we looked through the collection, Michael said that, when it comes to his mother’s letters, he considers only two or three “really real”—that is, not written for public consumption. He has returned to one of these often.
paragraphs.zh: 去年夏天，罗比、迈克尔和我一起参观了波士顿大学图书馆的档案馆，那里收藏着他们父母许多监狱来信。我们翻看这些信件时，迈克尔说，谈到他母亲的信件时，他认为只有两三封才算“真正真实”的——也就是没有写给公众看的。他反复回到其中一封。

paragraphs.en: Two days before the scheduled executions, Robby and Michael visited their parents for the last time. Michael left crying and screaming: “One more day to live, one more day to live!” But Ethel didn’t cry. Later, she tried to explain her behavior in a note she sent her sons via her lawyer. To show emotion “would have been so easy, far too easy on myself,” she told them. “I took the hard way instead of the easy, because I love you more than myself and because I knew you needed that love far more than I needed the relief of crying.” She continued:
paragraphs.zh: 在预定处决前两天，罗比和迈克尔最后一次探望了父母。迈克尔离开时哭喊着说：“再活一天，再活一天！”但伊塞尔没有哭。后来，她通过律师给儿子们写了一封信，试图解释她的举动。她对他们说：“流露情绪本来是很容易的，对我来说太容易了。” “我走的是艰难的路，而不是轻松的路，因为我比任何人都更爱你们，因为我知道你们更需要这种爱，而不是我需要哭泣带来的轻松。” 她继续写道：

paragraphs.en: I know, sweethearts, an explanation of this kind cannot ever substitute for what we have been missing and for what we hope to be able to return to, nor do I intend it as any such thing. Only, as I say, we need to try to remain calm and free from panic so that we can do all we can to help one another to see this thing through!
paragraphs.zh: 我知道，亲爱的孩子们，这种解释永远不能代替我们所失去的，也不能代替我们希望能够挽回的一切，我也不打算把它当成那种东西。只是在此，我要说的是，我们需要尽量保持冷静，不要惊慌，这样我们才能尽我们所能互相帮助，共同把这件事扛过去。

paragraphs.en: Just after 8 p.m. on June 19, Julius followed Sing Sing’s Jewish chaplain to the electric chair. As the rabbi prayed, the executioner secured the five leather straps. Before he delivered the fatal shocks, he put a leather face mask on Julius meant to prevent his eyes from popping out of his head. Julius was pronounced dead after he received three charges of electricity.
paragraphs.zh: 6月19日晚上8点刚过，朱利叶斯跟着辛辛监狱的犹太教牧师来到电椅前。牧师在祈祷时，刽子手给朱利叶斯系上了五道皮带。在施以致命电击之前，他给朱利叶斯戴上了一个皮制面罩，目的是防止他眼球脱出。朱利叶斯在接受三次电击后被宣布死亡。

paragraphs.en: The rabbi then gave Ethel a final chance to live. Did she have any names to share? She said she did not: “I’m innocent; I’m prepared to die.” She was strapped in and masked. The executioner delivered three shocks. But doctors found that the three charges had not been sufficient; Ethel’s heart was still beating. It took two more jolts, and four and a half minutes total, to kill her.
paragraphs.zh: 随后，拉比给了埃塞尔最后一次活命的机会。她问她是否有要认领的名字。埃塞尔说她没有：“我无辜，我已经准备好去死了。”她被绑好并蒙上了面罩。刽子手放出了三次电击。但医生发现这三次电流还不够，埃塞尔的心脏还在跳动。最终又给了两次电击，总共四分半钟后，她才停止呼吸。

paragraphs.en: Michael and Robby were watching a baseball game on TV when a news flash came on the screen announcing that their parents were about to be executed. The adults sent the boys out to play ball with friends. By the time they came back in, they were orphans.
paragraphs.zh: 迈克尔和罗比正在看电视上的一场棒球比赛时，新闻快讯出现在屏幕上，播报说他们的父母即将被执行死刑。大人们把男孩们送出去和朋友们一起去打球。等他们回来时，自己成了孤儿。

paragraphs.en: I first read Robby’s memoir, An Execution in the Family, soon after it was published, in 2004. I was 11 then, and gripped by the kid’s-eye perspective of the early chapters. I retained two distinct mental images from the book. One was of the Hebrew Children’s Home. The other was of a train set, which served as a partial answer to the question of how the boys could have turned out as well-adjusted as they had; there was, strange as it sounds, a happy part of this story.
paragraphs.zh: 我在2004年这本书出版不久后第一次读了罗比的回忆录《一次家庭处决》。那时我11岁，被书前几章里孩子视角的叙事牢牢吸引。书里我留下了两个清晰的画面。一个是希伯来儿童之家；另一个则是一套玩具火车轨道，它在某种程度上回答了一个问题：这些男孩为什么会长得像样、过得这么正常；听起来很奇怪，故事里其实有一段快乐的部分。

paragraphs.en: On Christmas Eve 1953, the boys went to a party at the home of W. E. B. Du Bois, who had advocated for clemency for Ethel and Julius. Inside the largest house the boys had ever been in was an enormous Christmas tree with a pile of presents underneath it, all for them. This was where Michael and Robby first met Anne and Abel Meeropol, who asked them that night if they wanted to move in with them. Their own two sons had been stillborn, and Anne and Abel, now in their 40s, wanted badly to be parents.
paragraphs.zh: 1953年圣诞前夜，那两个男孩受邀参加了一场聚会，地点是W. E. B. 杜波依斯的家。杜波依斯当时曾为埃塞尔和朱利叶斯大力请求减刑。在他们有生以来见过的最大的一幢房子里，摆着一棵巨大的圣诞树，树下堆满了礼物，都是给他们的。那天夜里，迈克尔和罗比第一次见到了安妮和阿贝尔·梅罗波尔。安妮和阿贝尔问他们是否愿意搬去和他们一起住。那对夫妇的亲生儿子当时已经死胎，安妮和阿贝尔都已进入40多岁，却非常渴望成为父母。

paragraphs.en: By the start of 1954, Robby and Michael were living with the Meeropols in Manhattan; the couple were fun and loving, and the boys quickly began calling them Mommy and Daddy. But one night in mid-February, after Robby had gone to sleep, New York City police officers knocked on the door of the couple’s apartment to demand that he and Michael be turned over immediately.
paragraphs.zh: 到1954年初，罗比和迈克尔与米罗波尔一家住在曼哈顿；这对夫妇性格随和、充满爱心，两个男孩很快就开始叫他们“妈妈”和“爸爸”。但在2月中旬的一个夜里，罗比入睡后，纽约警察敲响了这对夫妇公寓的门，要求立即把罗比和迈克尔交出来。

paragraphs.en: Ethel and Julius’s lawyer had died of a heart attack before he could legally transfer guardianship to the Meeropols. Now two child-welfare groups had obtained a court order to remove Michael and Robby from the Meeropols’ home, on the grounds that living with former Communist Party members was not in the boys’ best interest. Abel refused to open the door and, in Michael’s telling, informed the officers that, if they wanted to take Robby and Michael, they’d have to kill him first. Somehow, he and Anne were able to arrange with lawyers to keep the boys overnight.
paragraphs.zh: 伊瑟尔和朱利叶斯的律师在尚未能依法把监护权正式转移给米罗波尔一家之前，就因心脏病发作去世了。现在，两家儿童福利机构已拿到法院命令，要求把迈克尔和罗比从米罗波尔家中带走，理由是让他们与前共产党党员一起生活不符合两个男孩的最佳利益。按迈克尔的说法，阿贝尔拒绝开门，并告诉警察说：如果他们想带走罗比和迈克尔，就得先把他杀了。后来，阿贝尔和安妮设法和律师协商，让兄弟俩那晚能够留下来。

paragraphs.en: The next day, a judge ruled that Robby and Michael should be considered wards of the state and sent them to an orphanage. But the day after that, the Meeropols and Sophie Rosenberg, the boys’ grandmother, appealed to the New York Supreme Court, which gave Sophie temporary custody. For the next several months, Robby lived in fear of being sent back to the orphanage. Eventually, in September 1954, he and Michael were allowed to return to the Meeropols’. Recounting this turning point as we pored over the archives at Boston University, Michael began to cry. Anne later told him, he said, that she and Abel had plans to flee to the Soviet Union with the boys if they were not granted custody.
paragraphs.zh: 第二天，法官裁定罗比和迈克尔应被视为国家监护人下的未成年人，并将其送往孤儿院。但第二天，米罗波尔一家与孩子们的祖母索菲亚·罗森伯格向纽约州最高法院提出上诉，法院临时将监护权授予索菲。接下来几个月，罗比始终害怕被送回孤儿院。最终，1954年9月，他们被允许返回米罗波尔家。在我们于波士顿大学检索档案时，迈克尔回忆起这段转折时不禁哭了出来。后来，安妮告诉他说，她和阿贝尔曾计划，如果未能获得监护权，就带着孩子们逃往苏联。

paragraphs.en: Michael and Robby started using the Meeropol name almost immediately, allowing them at last to slip back into private life, and they were formally adopted in 1957. The family’s Washington Heights apartment was, as Michael and Robby remember it, always full of laughter, music, and funny voices; it had room for all of their toys, including a Lionel electric train set. The boys kept huge tanks of fish and had a cat named Fuzzy Shnooky Romeo. When Robby went away to camp, Abel drew him cartoon postcards with silly, smiling animals. “Daddy got us worms!” the fish exclaim in one. “Yum! Yum! Hooray!” Abel signed the postcards “Pop.” Robby still has them.
paragraphs.zh: 罗比和迈克尔几乎立刻开始使用“米罗波尔”这个姓氏，这让他们终于得以重新回归私生活，并于1957年正式完成收养。这对家庭在华盛顿高地的公寓，在他们的记忆里，总是充满笑声、音乐和各种搞笑的声音；那里有足够的空间放下他们所有玩具，包括一套利昂内尔电动火车。孩子们养着巨大的鱼缸，家里还养了一只叫“肥嘟嘟·雪纳奇·罗密欧”的猫。罗比去夏令营时，阿贝尔会给他画卡通明信片，里面有些滑稽又笑咪咪的动物。有一张上面写着：“爸爸给我们买了蚯蚓！”“好吃！好吃！太好了！”阿贝尔在明信片上签上“爸爸”。罗比现在还留着这些明信片。

paragraphs.en: In 1954, Robby and Michael went to live in Manhattan with Anne and Abel Meeropol, who later became their adoptive parents. (Christopher Churchill for The Atlantic; courtesy of the Meeropol family)
paragraphs.zh: 1954年，罗比和迈克尔与安妮和阿贝尔·米洛波尔一起住进了曼哈顿。后来，安妮和阿贝尔正式成为他们的养父母。（克里斯托弗·丘吉尔发表于《大西洋月刊》；经米洛波尔一家许可）

paragraphs.en: Both Abel and Anne had been public-school teachers; Abel had taught James Baldwin high-school English. Abel was also a songwriter—in the 1930s, he’d written the music and lyrics to “Strange Fruit,” a song about lynching made popular by Billie Holiday—and had been successful enough that the couple could now live primarily off his royalties, in effect serving as full-time parents.
paragraphs.zh: 阿贝尔和安妮都曾在公立学校任教；阿贝尔还曾教过詹姆斯·鲍德温高中英语。阿贝尔还是一名词曲作者——1930年代，他为《奇异的果实》（《Strange Fruit》）写了词和曲，这首歌讲述了私刑私吊（私刑致死）并由比莉·哈乐迪演唱走红——并且已经取得了足够的成功，使这对夫妇现在主要靠他的版税生活，实际上相当于全职父母。

paragraphs.en: Anne and Abel rarely brought up the past with Michael and Robby, but when Ethel and Julius did come up, the Meeropols reinforced their sons’ impression of them as brave, principled people who had been wrongly executed. As they grew into adulthood, Michael and Robby came to treat that final letter from June 1953 as gospel: Always remember that we were innocent and could not wrong our conscience.
paragraphs.zh: 安妮和阿贝尔很少和迈克尔、罗比提起过去，但一旦谈到伊瑟尔和朱利叶斯，米洛波尔一家就会不断加深他们的印象：他们认为这两人是勇敢而有原则的人，并且被错误地执行了死刑。随着迈克尔和罗比逐渐成人，他们把1953年6月的一封最后书信当作信条来对待：始终记住，我们是无辜的，不可能违背自己的良知。

paragraphs.en: In the years immediately following his parents’ executions, Michael said, he didn’t cry at all. But when he was 16, a work of fiction—a 1947 novel by Willard Motley—finally cracked the defensive shell he’d developed. In his journal, he wrote:
paragraphs.zh: 迈克尔说，在父母被处决后的几年里，他完全没有哭过。但在他16岁时，一本小说——威拉德·莫特利于1947年写作的作品——最终击碎了他已建立起的防御外壳。在日记里，他写道：

paragraphs.en: I read the end of Knock on Any Door which describes a man’s feelings as he goes to his death in the chair. It shook me, and it broke me. I realized that the two dearest people in the world to me had gone through that agony and more. They probably worried about their “babies” and what would happen to them. My God was it awful. I cried and cried and cried so much. I feel terrible.
paragraphs.zh: 我读完了《敲开任何一扇门》的结尾，里面描写了一个人走向电椅前往死路的心情。那一段把我震撼住了，也击碎了我。我意识到，对我来说世界上最亲爱的人曾经历过那种痛苦，甚至更甚。他们一定很担心自己的“宝宝”，以及他们的命运会怎样。我的天啊，太可怕了。我一直在哭，哭了又哭，哭得停不下来。我感觉糟透了。

paragraphs.en: Oh to be half as courageous as those two wonderful people—Ethel and Julius Rosenberg. MY GOD
paragraphs.zh: 啊，真希望自己能有这两位了不起的夫妻——艾瑟尔和朱利叶斯·罗森伯格——那样一半的勇气。我的天啊。

paragraphs.en: Abel saw him crying, and saw what he’d been reading, and gave him a long hug.
paragraphs.zh: 阿贝尔看到他在哭，看到了他正在读的内容，于是给了他一个长长的拥抱。

paragraphs.en: More than their childhood, Robby and Michael wanted to talk with me about the chapter of their lives that began in the 1970s, when they chose to “come out” as Rosenbergs after years of keeping quiet about who they were.
paragraphs.zh: 比起他们的童年，罗比和迈克尔更想和我谈论他们人生中的这一章——这段生活在20世纪70年代开启，当时他们在多年的隐姓埋名之后，选择“公开亮相”为罗森伯格。

paragraphs.en: One night in early 1973, Michael heard that a trial lawyer named Louis Nizer, who had not been involved in the Rosenberg case, was reading out loud from his parents’ prison letters on TV. Nizer had just published a popular book about the case, The Implosion Conspiracy, which concluded that the Rosenbergs were guilty as charged. Michael called Robby, upset. The brothers believed that they held the copyright to their parents’ letters, which they saw as their sole inheritance. Why should Nizer be allowed to use them for his own biased ends?
paragraphs.zh: 1973年初的一个夜晚，迈克尔听说一位名叫路易斯·奈泽（Louis Nizer）的审判律师在电视上大声朗读了他们父母在狱中的信件。奈泽并未参与罗斯堡案，但他刚刚出版了一本关于该案的热门书《引爆阴谋》（The Implosion Conspiracy），并得出结论：罗斯堡夫妇应负全责。迈克尔很生气地打电话给罗比。两兄弟认为自己拥有父母书信的版权，而那也是他们唯一的遗产。他们不明白，为什么奈泽可以把这些信件拿来为自己的偏见目的服务。

paragraphs.en: On June 19, 1973, the 20th anniversary of their parents’ deaths, they filed a lawsuit. “We are strongly reaffirming our identification with and respect for our murdered parents and what they did for us both in their role as citizens as well as in their relationship with us as parents,” they wrote. It wasn’t long before the press showed up. “All of a sudden we were public,” Robby recalled. “And the sky didn’t fall.” In some ways, it was liberating to no longer be keeping such a big secret.
paragraphs.zh: 1973年6月19日，在父母去世20周年之际，他们提起了诉讼。"我们再次强调，我们与自己被杀害的父母站在一起，并尊敬他们；他们既是公民，也作为父母在我们人生中都做了自己认为该做的事。"他们这样写道。不久后，媒体蜂拥而至。罗比回忆道："转眼之间，我们成了公众人物。" "天并没有塌下来。" 在某种程度上，能不再长期背负一个巨大的秘密，反而让他们感到一种解放。

paragraphs.en: The legal bills were expensive, and they began traveling around the country to raise money, giving speeches under the auspices of a new National Committee to Reopen the Rosenberg Case. They planned to use the Freedom of Information Act to obtain the government’s evidence, or lack thereof, about their parents. In 1974, Robby stood onstage at Carnegie Hall and declared, “In the next year we are going to blow the lid on this case.”
paragraphs.zh: 诉讼费用高昂，他们便开始在全国各地奔走演讲筹款，并在一个新成立的"重新开启罗斯堡案全国委员会"（National Committee to Reopen the Rosenberg Case）的名义下开展活动。他们计划借助《信息自由法》获得政府掌握的、关于其父母的证据，或至少查明其中是否缺少证据。1974年，罗比站上卡内基音乐厅舞台，宣布道："明年我们将掀开这个案件的盖子。"

paragraphs.en: Touring the country, they met well-wishers who were emotional about seeing “the boys” as grown men, people who cried and hugged them and hung around a little too long. Robby and Michael found this draining. Their preferred approach relied more on logic than emotion. They vowed to follow the facts. They would become experts; they would make an unimpeachable case. They wouldn’t get carried away by anger or grief.
paragraphs.zh: 在全国巡回演讲中，他们遇到了不少善意者，这些人为在成长后的“男孩们”而流泪、拥抱，且往往逗留得有些过久。罗比和迈克尔觉得这很消耗精力。相比感情，他们更偏好理性的做法。他们发誓要循着事实走。他们要成为专家；他们要做出一个不容置疑的案证。他们不让自己被愤怒或悲伤牵着走。

paragraphs.en: Still, revisiting the letters and reading the trial transcripts for the first time forced Michael to relive the pain of his parents’ deaths, he told me. Publicly, he remained composed, but in private, for a year or so, he “cried, cursed, raged,” struck anew by the pathos of his parents’ correspondence and the could-have-beens of their complex appeals process. By the late 1970s, the brothers were exhausted by the work of being professional Rosenbergs, and starting to worry that the effort was futile. Their FOIA lawsuit had yielded hundreds of thousands of documents, but there was no smoking gun—maybe there never would be.
paragraphs.zh: 不过，迈克尔告诉我，说起重读这些信件并首次阅读审判记录，让他再次重温了父母去世带来的痛苦。公开场合他始终保持冷静，但在私下里，大约持续了一年，他“哭泣、咒骂、狂怒”，再次被父母书信中那种悲怆以及他们复杂上诉程序中一连串可能性（却最终未能实现的）所深深击中。到1970年代后期，兄弟俩已被长期扮演“职业罗森堡人”这件事耗尽了精力，并开始担心这种努力是徒劳的。他们依据《信息自由法》(FOIA)提起的诉讼虽然获得了数十万份文件，但并未出现“定罪证据”，也许这种东西本来就不会出现。

paragraphs.en: From the October 1983 issue: The Rosenberg file
paragraphs.zh: 1983年10月号：《罗森堡档案》

paragraphs.en: Sometimes, Robby and Michael told me, they wondered if there might be more to their parents’ story than what was in their letters. They never questioned that Ethel and Julius had been framed as atomic spies, but could they have committed some lesser offense that had made them government targets? Yet whenever the brothers asked Morton Sobell, their parents’ co-defendant, to tell them the truth, all they got was the same one-line answer: They were innocent.
paragraphs.zh: 有时，罗比和迈克尔告诉我，他们曾想，也许父母的故事不仅仅是信里写下的内容。他们从未怀疑伊瑟尔和朱利叶斯被当作核武间谍而受审，但他们可能犯了更轻微的罪行，使得自己成为政府盯上的对象吗？然而每次他们向父母的共同被告莫顿·索贝尔询问真相，得到的都只是一句同样的回应：他们是无辜的。

paragraphs.en: With the collapse of the Soviet Union, rumors started trickling out of Russia: According to erstwhile KGB agents now speaking openly for the first time, Julius Rosenberg had in fact been a spy, and a valuable one. But Soviet intelligence operatives aren’t anyone’s idea of reliable sources, and it was easy for Robby and Michael to brush their claims aside.
paragraphs.zh: 随着苏联解体，关于俄罗斯的传闻开始逐渐传出：有前克格勃特工首次公开发言称，朱利叶斯·罗森堡确实是一名间谍，而且是个很有价值的间谍。但苏联情报人员向来不是任何人都能信任的消息来源，罗比和迈克尔很容易把这些说法搁置一旁。

paragraphs.en: In July 1995, the U.S. government released a series of previously classified documents known as the Venona files. These decrypted Soviet communications from the 1940s told a similar story to that of the former KGB agents: Julius, along with Ruth and David Greenglass, had spied for the Soviet Union. The headlines crowed with the certainty of a fresh verdict: “NEW EVIDENCE PROVES COUPLE WERE SPIES, OFFICIALS SAY.”
paragraphs.zh: 1995年7月，美国政府公开了一批此前被列为机密的文件，即“维诺纳档案”。这些解密的苏联通讯资料重申了前苏联特工的故事：朱利叶斯与鲁斯和大卫·格林格拉斯一起为苏联从事情报活动。新闻标题充满了“定论”般的确信："新证据证明这对夫妇是间谍，官员表示。"

paragraphs.en: To Robby and Michael, suddenly seeing their parents in the news again as spies was painful. The whole thing felt like a disaster—“like a ton of bricks,” as Robby put it to me. In a joint statement, they suggested that the NSA and the CIA had “cooked” the evidence to frame their parents. Venona, they insisted, changed none of their conclusions. One official release was not going to be enough to undo years of deep suspicion of the government.
paragraphs.zh: 对罗比和迈克尔来说，重新在新闻里看到父母被当作间谍，是突如其来的痛苦。整件事让他们感觉像一场灾难——正如罗比对我说的，"像一桶砖头掉下来"。在一份联合声明中，他们暗示国家安全局和中央情报局"炮制"证据、栽赃了自己的父母。罗比和迈克尔坚持认为，维诺纳并没有改变他们对这件事的任何结论。一次官方声明不足以扭转多年形成的政府深度怀疑。

paragraphs.en: Still, Robby and Michael parsed the new evidence carefully. The files, they noticed, contained only one mention of Ethel: She was politically aligned with her husband and aware of his activities, the decrypted text said, but “in view of delicate health does not work.” This seemed like an important clue. If you interpreted “work” to mean “spy,” as Robby and Michael did, that would suggest the damning typing story was pure invention on the Greenglasses’ part.
paragraphs.zh: 尽管如此，罗比和迈克尔还是仔细梳理了新证据。文件里只出现了一个与艾瑟尔相关的线索：解密文字写道，她与丈夫政治立场一致，也知道他的活动，但“考虑到她身体较弱，不参与工作”。这看起来是个关键线索。如果你把“工作”理解为“间谍活动”，正如罗比和迈克尔所做的那样，就意味着那段指控性的打字机证词可能完全是格林格拉斯兄妹捏造的。

paragraphs.en: Ethel’s brother David Greenglass told authorities that he had been part of a spy ring led by Julius Rosenberg. (Ruth Morgan / New York Daily News Archive / Getty)
paragraphs.zh: 艾瑟尔的兄弟大卫·格林格拉斯向当局作证，称他曾是由朱利叶斯·罗斯堡领导的间谍网的一员。（鲁思·摩根 / 纽约每日新闻档案 / 盖蒂）

paragraphs.en: Slowly, more clues emerged. In 1999, a former KGB agent published a book revealing findings from Soviet archives that had briefly been opened a few years earlier. This, too, implicated Julius as a spy. Then, in a 2001 TV interview, David Greenglass (who had been living under an assumed name since he got out of prison and appeared on the program in disguise) admitted that he’d lied on the stand when he said that Ethel had typed his notes. “I don’t know who typed it, frankly,” he said. “To this day, I can’t even remember that the typing took place.”
paragraphs.zh: 线索渐渐出现。1999年，一名前克格勃特工发表了一本书，披露了几年前曾短暂开放过的苏联档案中的发现。这同样牵连了朱利叶斯。随后，在一次2001年的电视采访中，大卫·格林格拉斯（他自出狱后一直用化名生活，并在节目中伪装着出场）承认自己在证词中撒过谎，说伊瑟尔打过他的笔记这一点就是假的。“坦白说，我不知道是谁打的字。”他说，“直到今天，我甚至想不起当时确实发生过打字这件事。”

paragraphs.en: Greenglass did not express remorse for having sent his sister to her death, citing her “stupidity” for not cooperating with authorities. He said his conscience was clean. When the interviewer asked him what he would say to Robby and Michael, whom he had not seen since they were children, he replied, “I’m sorry that your parents are dead.”
paragraphs.zh: 格林格拉斯并未对把他姐姐送上刑场表示悔恨，他把不配合当局归咎于她的“愚蠢”。他说自己的良心是清白的。当采访者问他如果见到罗比和迈克尔会说些什么——他已经多年未见这对儿子，自他们还是孩子时便未再相见——他回答道：“我很抱歉，你们的父母已经去世。”

paragraphs.en: Morton Sobell was the one person who could definitively answer Robby and Michael’s questions about their parents. He had been Julius’s friend since they’d met as engineering students at City College. He had stood trial with both Rosenbergs and, after getting out of prison in 1969, stayed in touch with Robby and Michael.
paragraphs.zh: 莫顿·索贝尔是唯一能够明确回答罗比和迈克尔关于父母问题的人。朱利叶斯和他是在纽约城市学院作为工程专业学生相识后结为朋友。他和罗森堡夫妇一起受审，并在1969年出狱后，一直与罗比和迈克尔保持联系。

paragraphs.en: For nearly six decades, Sobell maintained his innocence. But in 2008, at the age of 91, he told a reporter that, yes, he and Julius had turned over military secrets to the Soviet Union during World War II. If you wanted to call that spying, then sure, they’d been spies. Ethel, he said, had known what Julius was doing. “But what was she guilty of? Of being Julius’s wife.” Robby and Michael felt as though, for the first time, a trusted source had stated it plainly: Julius was a spy, and Ethel was not.
paragraphs.zh: 近六十年来，索贝尔始终坚持自己的清白立场。但在2008年，91岁那年，他告诉一位记者说，没错，在二战期间，他和朱利叶斯确实把军事机密交给了苏联。如果你想称那是间谍活动，那么可以说，他们确实是间谍。索贝尔说，伊瑟尔知道朱利叶斯在做什么。“那她到底有什么罪？不过是朱利叶斯的妻子。”罗比和迈克尔第一次感觉到，终于有一个值得信赖的消息源将事实说得清清楚楚：朱利叶斯是间谍，而伊瑟尔不是。

paragraphs.en: The brothers were shocked by Sobell’s sudden admission. They had asked him again and again to tell them the truth about their parents, even if only in private. Why had he lied? Michael still thinks about this, he said: “What if Morty had been honest with us?” He could have spared them so many years of uncertainty.
paragraphs.zh: 当索贝尔突然坦白时，兄弟俩都非常震惊。他们一次又一次地求他，即使只是在私下，也告诉他们有关父母的真相。他为什么要撒谎？迈克尔说，他至今还在想这件事：“如果莫提一开始就对我们诚实，结果会怎样？”他本可以替他们省下许多年反复猜测的痛苦。

paragraphs.en: Even so, the brothers told me that finally learning the truth was a relief. They were in their 60s by then—both grandfathers—and had had more than a decade to consider the information in Venona. They now understood that their parents’ prosecution hadn’t been a senseless witch hunt, as they’d once believed. Ethel and Julius hadn’t been targeted randomly, or simply for having been Jewish Communists during the McCarthy era. And because Sobell himself had been the one to say it publicly, Robby and Michael wouldn’t have to do the uncomfortable job of breaking the news to the remaining Rosenberg true believers, who still thought that any talk of spying was a government hoax.
paragraphs.zh: 尽管如此，兄弟俩还是告诉我，最终知道真相让他们松了口气。到那时他们都已进入六十多岁，都是祖父级的人了，已经有十多年时间来消化维诺纳档案中的信息。他们现在明白，父母的起诉并不是毫无意义的猎巫行动，正如他们曾经所相信的那样。伊瑟尔和朱利叶斯并非被随机盯上，更不是因为他们在麦卡锡时代是犹太裔共产党员而遭到针对。而且，因为索贝尔本人公开承认了这件事，罗比和迈克尔不必去向剩下那些仍然“罗斯伯格”真信者传达这个消息——那些人仍觉得任何有关间谍活动的说法都只是政府编造的阴谋。

paragraphs.en: I was taken aback by their matter-of-factness. Was that really it? Could their ultimate reaction to learning that Julius had spied for the Soviet Union have been gratitude that they didn’t have to be the messengers? I wanted to know how they felt about the actual spying—about the fact that their father had shared highly classified information in order to aid another, later hostile, country’s militarization, destroying their family in the process.
paragraphs.zh: 他们这种平静坦然让我有些意外。这真是全部吗？当他们得知朱利叶斯曾为苏联从事情报活动后，最深层的反应真的会是“不用我们去当传话人”这份如释重负的情绪吗？我想知道他们如何看待真正的间谍行为——也就是他们的父亲把高度机密信息交给后来敌对的国家，用于助力其军备化建设，并在这个过程中摧毁了自己家庭的事实。

paragraphs.en: When I pressed them on this, Michael and Robby conceded that perhaps Julius had been, as Robby put it, “kind of a cowboy,” prone to taking big risks without much concern for the effect they might have. Maybe, Michael suggested, a more responsible choice for a spy would have been to not have children at all. Nonetheless, they both remain sympathetic toward their father, and continue to argue that he was unfairly prosecuted. Even if Julius was a spy, they maintain, he was not an atomic spy, and as such was not guilty of the charges for which he was executed.
paragraphs.zh: 当我追问到这里时，迈克尔和罗比承认，也许朱利叶斯确实像罗比说的那样，“有点像个牛仔”，习惯于不太在意自己的行为可能带来的影响就赌一把冒大风险。也许，迈克尔提出过，作为一个间谍，更负责任的做法也许是干脆不要再有孩子。尽管如此，他们两人仍对父亲保持同情，并且一直认为他受到了不公正的起诉。即使朱利叶斯是间谍，他们也坚持认为，他并不是原子间谍，因此也不该被判犯有处决时所指控的罪名。

paragraphs.en: Julius seems to have approached his spying with a sprightly, if naive, enthusiasm. He was 24 and working as a junior engineer for the Army Signal Corps in New York when he was recruited by Russian agents in 1942, drawn to the promise of helping defeat Hitler and the notion, however fanciful, that the Soviet Union represented a solution to America’s social ills. He went on to provide thousands of documents containing valuable military-industrial information, and became an active recruiter. His Soviet handler Alexander Feklisov recalled in his memoir that Julius could sometimes “be as carefree as a teenager,” such as when he greeted the Russian during an early street-corner meeting with a hearty “Hello comrade!” On another occasion, Feklisov wrote, Julius told him that, although he loved his wife and son (this was before Robby was born), his meetings with Feklisov were “among the happiest moments of my life.”
paragraphs.zh: 朱利叶斯接触间谍活动时似乎带着一种轻快但有些幼稚的热情。1942年，他24岁，在纽约担任陆军通信兵团的初级工程师时被苏联特工招募了，当时他被战胜希特勒的目标吸引，并且相信（尽管这种想法有些天真）苏联是美国社会弊病的一种解决方案。他后来提供了成千上万份含有珍贵军工情报的文件，并成为一名积极的内应。苏联线人亚历山大·费克利索夫在回忆录中写道，朱利叶斯有时“可以像个十几岁的少年一样无忧无虑”，比如在一次街头拐角处的早期会面中，他热情地对苏联人说了一句“同志，你好！”。费克利索夫又写到，另一次朱利叶斯告诉他，虽然他深爱着妻子和儿子（那时罗比还没出生），但和费克利索夫见面仍是“我一生中最快乐的时刻之一”。

paragraphs.en: Unlike Julius, Ethel was never given a code name in Venona, which Michael and Robby took to mean that she was not a spy. Why, then, should she have sacrificed herself, leaving her sons behind, particularly after Julius was executed and she was given one last chance to cooperate? Why did she choose death?
paragraphs.zh: 与朱利叶斯不同，伊瑟尔在维诺娜计划中从未被赋予代号，因此迈克尔和罗比认为这意味着她并不是间谍。那为什么她会选择牺牲自己，留下两个儿子，尤其是当朱利叶斯已被处决、而她还曾得到过最后一次合作机会时？她为什么会选择死亡？

paragraphs.en: The question nagged at me for months; no single explanation—psychological, political, or otherwise—felt completely satisfying. Part of me wanted to see Ethel as a victim, caught between her husband’s recklessness, the vindictiveness of powerful men who didn’t care whether she lived or died, and the fear that she was already too far down a path of deception to allow the world, let alone her sons, to learn the messy truth. I thought about the toll that years of what was essentially solitary confinement, as the only woman on death row, would take on anyone; it seemed likely that she was not, by the end, thinking rationally. But that way of looking at it denied Ethel agency. It was possible, of course, that she’d made the choice to die of her own free will—as a martyr for the Communist cause, to make the United States look bad, out of loyalty to Julius.
paragraphs.zh: 我把这个问题困扰了好几个月；任何单一的解释——无论是心理上的、政治上的，还是其他方面的——都觉得不完全令人满意。我一方面想把伊瑟尔看作一个受害者：被丈夫的轻率、权势男子的报复心所夹在中间，这些人不在乎她生还是死，也害怕她已经走上了一条充满欺骗的道路，无法让世界，别说她的两个儿子，去了解那混乱真实的真相。我也想过，长期几乎被单独关押在死囚牢房里，这种处境对任何人都会造成怎样的压力；到了最后，她很可能已不再清醒理性地思考。然而，这种看法又否认了伊瑟尔的主体性。当然，也完全可能她是出于自己的意志选择了死亡——为了共产主义事业成为烈士、为了让美国看起来很差、为了对朱利叶斯的忠诚。

paragraphs.en: When I raised the subject with Robby and Michael, they readily agreed that Ethel could have saved herself, had she wanted to. “She did have names,” Robby said. “In fact, if she’d given the names of Sarant and Barr”—Alfred Sarant and Joel Barr, members of the spy ring who had already defected to the Soviet Union—“nothing would have happened.”
paragraphs.zh: 当我和罗比、迈克尔提起这个话题时，他们都很快同意，伊瑟尔本可以在想要的时候救自己。罗比说：“她确实知道一些人名。”他接着说：“其实，如果她当时把萨兰特和巴尔——阿尔弗雷德·萨兰特和乔尔·巴尔——这两个已经逃往苏联的间谍组织成员的名字都说出来了，什么都不会发生。”

paragraphs.en: “So should she have?” I asked.
paragraphs.zh: “那么她应该这样做吗？”我问。

paragraphs.en: Both brothers replied immediately and emphatically. “Oh no,” Michael said. “I don’t see why,” Robby added. Making any choice other than the one she ultimately made, they argued, would have left Ethel with a permanent sense of guilt over turning on her husband and reversing her steadfast commitment to “see this thing through”—the battle she’d been waging, and the story of innocence she’d been telling her sons and the world for three years.
paragraphs.zh: 两兄弟立即而坚定地回答。“哦，不，”迈克尔说。“我不明白为什么要这么做，”罗比补充道。他们认为，如果做出其他任何选择，不是她最终做出的那个选择，就会让伊塞尔长期带着内疚感，觉得自己背叛了丈夫，背离了她坚持到底的承诺；那场她一直在战斗的斗争，和她过去三年里对儿子们和世界讲述的无辜故事就会被推翻。

paragraphs.en: “Mom could’ve said, I knew Julius was a spy; I helped him a little bit. I knew the names of two of the people he worked with. That’s all I know; take it or leave it,” Michael said. “They would’ve taken it.”
paragraphs.zh: “妈妈本可以说：我知道朱利叶斯是间谍；我帮了他一点忙。我知道他共事过的两个人的名字。这就是我知道的全部，信就信，不信就算，”迈克尔说。“他们会接受的。”

paragraphs.en: “Who knows,” Robby muttered, but Michael’s counterfactual scenario seemed plausible enough to me, and I let him keep talking as he imagined what might have happened next.
paragraphs.zh: “谁知道呢，”罗比嘀咕着，但迈克尔的这种假设情景在我看来似乎足够可信，我就让他继续讲下去，想象接下来可能会发生什么。

paragraphs.en: “She would have served some time in prison, and repudiated her marriage, and repudiated all the people who had supported them, and pulled the rug out from under Rob and me when they said that ‘we were innocent.’ And then shown up in our lives.”
paragraphs.zh: “她本该服刑一段时间，否认她的婚姻，否认所有曾支持他们的人，在他们说‘我们是无辜的’时，当着我们的面抽走我们依靠的地基。然后又突然重新闯入我们的生活。”

paragraphs.en: I knew he was trying to explain why this never would have worked. But even if their mother reappearing in their lives at a later date might have proved complicated, it was hard for me to believe that it would truly have been worse than not having her in their lives at all. “We might have grown up to hate her,” Michael continued. He wasn’t sure he could have forgiven her for turning on his father—or for exposing the reality, while she was still alive, that both parents had lied to their sons. “The thought of her living that life was so horrible that I think it was easier to just follow him into the grave.”
paragraphs.zh: 我知道他是在说明这件事为什么根本不可能实现。但即使母亲在后来再度出现在他们生活中，可能会带来复杂局面，我也很难相信，这真会比她根本不在他们生活中更糟。“我们可能会长大后恨她，”迈克尔继续说。他不确定自己是否能原谅她背叛了父亲——或者在她还活着时就揭穿了现实：这两个父母都对他们的儿子们撒了谎。“想到她还活着要过那样的生活，太可怕了，所以我想跟他一同走入坟墓会更容易。”

paragraphs.en: Michael seemed to be saying that by admitting her dishonesty, Ethel would have ruined their childhood and poisoned any prospect of a future relationship with her. But wouldn’t some people see the fact that she’d died for a story she knew was false—something the brothers were acknowledging—as a greater betrayal? Michael and Robby didn’t seem willing to think about it that way. In their telling, Ethel had died, at least in part, to protect them.
paragraphs.zh: 迈克尔似乎是在说，承认她的欺骗会让埃塞尔毁掉他们的童年，并彻底坏掉他们未来与她重建关系的任何可能。但是，难道不会有人认为，她为一个她知道是假的故事而去世——正是这对兄弟正在承认的事实——不是更大的背叛吗？迈克尔和罗比似乎不愿意以那种方式去看这件事。在他们的叙述里，埃塞尔至少在某种程度上是为了保护他们而去世的。

paragraphs.en: Ethel at her arraignment in August 1950 (Hulton Archive / Getty)
paragraphs.zh: 1950年8月，埃塞尔在法庭宣判时（Hulton档案馆/盖蒂）

paragraphs.en: For the brothers to wish for any other outcome would be for them to wish for an entirely different life than the one they’ve had and, against all odds, mostly enjoyed. It would be to wish that they’d had a childhood with no Meeropols, and an adulthood with more secrecy, less purpose. Faced with something—someone—so ultimately unknowable, perhaps the best that anyone can do is decide on their own version of the truth and stick to it.
paragraphs.zh: 对这两个兄弟来说，想要任何其他结果，都等于希望拥有一段与他们所度过的生活完全不同的人生——而且尽管重重阻力，他们那段人生大体上是值得珍惜的。也就是说，他们想要有一个没有梅罗波尔家人（Meeropols）参与的童年，以及一个更隐秘、目标更少的成年生活。面对一个——或某个人——终究无法被彻底认知的东西，也许每个人能做的最好方式，正是自己决定一种“真相”并坚定地站在其中。

paragraphs.en: I am not, I realize, the first to raise these contradictions. The ghost of Ethel Rosenberg has haunted American life for decades, by turns exasperating and enthralling those—psychiatrists, playwrights, novelists, scholars, journalists, relatives—who have tried to wrap their arms around the meaning of her life and death.
paragraphs.zh: 我意识到，我并不是第一个提出这些矛盾的人。伊塞尔·罗森伯格的幽灵在美国社会中萦绕了几十年，它时而让精神病学家、剧作家、小说家、学者、记者和亲属们又恼火又着迷，这些人都试图把她的一生与死亡意义抱在怀里、理解透彻。

paragraphs.en: Few today would dispute that the government’s case against Ethel was less than airtight. In 2015, after David Greenglass died, his 1950 grand-jury testimony was unsealed. “I said before, and say it again, honestly, this is a fact: I never spoke to my sister about this at all,” Greenglass had said. If that was true, then he had likely perjured himself when later, at trial, he gave the evidence that led to Ethel’s conviction and sentencing. To some, Ethel’s alleged encouragement of David’s recruitment is evidence enough of her active role in the conspiracy to commit espionage of which she was found guilty. But Robby and Michael argue that knowledge of a conspiracy is not the same as participation in it—and that the prosecution failed to produce any proof she did participate, beyond what came directly from the Greenglasses’ unreliable accounts.
paragraphs.zh: 今天很少有人会否认，检方对伊塞尔的起诉并不牢固。2015年，在戴维·格林格拉斯去世后，他1950年提交的大陪审团证词被解封。证词中他曾说：我之前也说过，再说一遍，老实讲，事实是这样的：我当时根本没跟我妹妹提过这件事。若这句话属实，那么他后来在审判中所作的证词——也就是促成伊塞尔被定罪并判刑的关键证据——很可能是伪证。对一些人而言，伊塞尔据称鼓励戴维参军（招募）已足以证明她积极参与了间谍阴谋，但罗比和迈克尔认为，仅知道阴谋并不等于参与阴谋；而且检方未能拿出任何直接证据证明她有实际参与，除了一份又一份来自格林格拉斯一方并不可靠的陈述。

paragraphs.en: Whether or not you believe that the Rosenbergs deserved to die, there’s no denying that the punishment they received was unusually severe. Harry Gold was convicted of conspiracy to commit espionage and got out of prison in time to work as a clinical chemist in a Philadelphia pathology lab before he died at 60. Ruth Greenglass confessed to being a courier at Los Alamos and never spent a single night in jail. And, indeed, the case against Ethel was especially thin.
paragraphs.zh: 无论你是否相信罗森伯格夫妇应不应该被处死，都无法否认他们受到的惩罚异常严厉。哈里·戈德被判共谋从事间谍活动，在获释后赶在去世前（60岁）曾在费城一家病理实验室担任临床化学师。露丝·格林格拉斯承认自己在洛斯阿拉莫斯担任过信使，却从未在监狱里度过一个晚上。并且，确实，检方对伊塞尔的起诉基础尤为薄弱。

paragraphs.en: All of this ought, I think, to count for something in any honest assessment of the legal and ethical validity of the Rosenberg verdict. But I wasn’t sure that the injustice of the whole affair was enough to explain, logically or morally, the clarion confidence of Ethel’s claim that “I was innocent, my husband was innocent, and neither of us knew anything about espionage.” That was a lie.
paragraphs.zh: 我认为，这一切都应当在任何对罗森伯格判决的法律和伦理合理性进行公正评估时被认真算入其中。但我不确定，整件事的不公是否足以在逻辑或道德上解释伊瑟尔那种铿锵自信的说法：‘我是无辜的，我丈夫也是无辜的，我们俩都不知道任何间谍活动。’那其实是个谎言。

paragraphs.en: Last summer, I visited Robby in western Massachusetts, where we sat in his sunny home office as his elderly gray cat threatened to knock over our water glasses. On the wall next to Robby’s desk, I noticed a calendar from the Union of Concerned Scientists. The cartoon for that month showed a uniformed military official lounging coquettishly on a bed, phone in hand. The caption read: “No, you dismantle your nuclear arsenal first.”
paragraphs.zh: 去年夏天，我去马萨诸塞州西部拜访了罗比。我们坐在他阳光明媚的家庭办公室里，他那只年迈的灰猫正威胁着要撞翻我们面前的水杯。就在罗比书桌旁的墙上，我注意到一张“忧虑科学家联盟”的日历。那个当月的卡通画上，一个穿军装的军官懒散地倚在床上，手里拿着电话。标题写着：“不，你先把自己的核武库拆掉吧。”

paragraphs.en: Other walls displayed framed mementos from the Rosenberg Fund for Children, the nonprofit Robby started in 1990 that supports the children of progressive activists who have lost their livelihood, been discriminated against, faced prison time, or died as a result of their activism (my parents have been longtime supporters of the RFC). Pointing to the images in his office, Robby told me that the organization has allowed him to connect with hundreds of kids and teenagers who can relate to what he experienced. They have sometimes asked why he’s not angrier. His response has always been that he considers his work to be a form of “constructive revenge.”
paragraphs.zh: 房间的其他墙上还挂着罗森伯格儿童基金会（Rosenberg Fund for Children）的纪念影像。那是罗比在1990年创办的非营利组织，旨在支持那些因进步派倡议而失去生计、遭受歧视、被判入狱，或因此离世的活动人士家庭中的孩子（我的父母一直是RFC的长期支持者）。罗比指着办公室里的这些图片告诉我，这个组织让他能够与数百位能理解他经历的孩子和青少年建立联系。有人曾问他为什么不更愤怒。他的回答始终是，他把自己的工作视为一种“建设性的复仇”。

paragraphs.en: “My taste in vengeance is more intellectual than physical,” Michael wrote in the ’80s. Talking with him in the 2020s, I could see what he meant. Rather than cause for frustration, it seemed to be almost a source of comfort that there will always be another thread in the Rosenberg case to pull—more documents to uncover, more verbal combat to undertake.
paragraphs.zh: 迈克尔在20世纪80年代写道：“我的复仇取向更偏向智识层面，而不是肉体层面。”与他在2020年代交谈时，我明白了他的意思。与其说这是一种挫败感，不如说它几乎成了某种安慰：在罗森伯格案中总会有另一条线索可以去追查——还有更多文件可以发现，更多口头辩论可以发起。

paragraphs.en: Over the past decade, Robby and Michael have made a concerted push for Ethel’s exoneration. In December 2016, the brothers again stood outside the White House, this time to deliver a petition asking President Obama to exonerate their mother in light of the newly released grand-jury testimony. They held a photograph of themselves there as boys, delivering the plea to Eisenhower that went unanswered. This one did, too.
paragraphs.zh: 在过去的十年里，罗比和迈克尔一直在有组织地为埃塞尔争取平反。2016年12月，兄弟俩再次站在白宫外，这次是为了递交一份请愿书，恳请奥巴马总统在新披露的大陪审团证词背景下为母亲平反。那时他们手里也拿着自己童年时期的合影，向艾森豪威尔提出过呼吁，但当年没有得到回应。这一次也没有。

paragraphs.en: But a newly declassified document released toward the end of Joe Biden’s presidency once again gave them hope. In September 2024, the brothers received a copy, via their lawyer, of a handwritten 1950 memo by Meredith Gardner, the chief Venona code breaker. If the Venona files represented Gardner’s translated decryptions of the original Russian messages, this document, they believed, showed his own takeaways from those decryptions, and were thus a better reflection of the U.S. government’s top-level findings. Gardner wrote, for example, that Ruth Greenglass was known “to have been a Soviet agent.” Just below that are his notes on Ethel:
paragraphs.zh: 但在乔·拜登总统任期末期解密的一份新文件又让他们重新燃起了希望。2024年9月，兄弟俩通过律师收到一份1940年代末的手写备忘录，来自维诺娜（Venona）密码破译负责人默里迪丝·加德纳（Meredith Gardner）。如果说“维诺娜档案”代表了加德纳对原始俄文电报的译文，那么这份文件，他们认为，展示了他自己对那些译文的解读，因此更能反映美国政府高层的真实结论。加德纳例如写道，露丝·格林格拉斯（Ruth Greenglass）“已被认定为苏联特工”。在这一段下面，是他关于埃塞尔的记录：

paragraphs.en: MRS. JULIUS ROSENBERG—A message of 27 November 1944 stated that Mrs. Rosenberg was a party member, a devoted wife, and that she knew about her husbands work, but that due to ill health she did not engage in the work herself.
paragraphs.zh: “朱利叶斯·罗森伯格太太———— 1944年11月27日的一封电报写道：罗森伯格夫人是共产党党员，是位尽职的妻子，而且她知道丈夫所做的工作，但由于健康不佳，她本人并未参与这些工作。”

paragraphs.en: Unlike the earlier releases, which stated only that Ethel did not work, this one specified that she did not engage in the work—the spying.
paragraphs.zh: 与此前公开的版本不同，后者只写明埃塞尔“没有工作”，这次却明确写道，她没有亲自参与这些“工作”——也就是间谍活动。

paragraphs.en: To Michael and Robby, this was the smoking gun that they had spent 50 years hoping to find. The first time they read the memo, they told me, they high-fived; Michael cried. They called on the Biden administration to exonerate their mother. Again, they were disappointed.
paragraphs.zh: 对迈克尔和罗比来说，这就是他们花了50年希望找到的“决定性证据”。他们第一次读到这份备忘录时，告诉我说，他们击掌庆祝；迈克尔还哭了。随后他们呼吁拜登政府为他们的母亲平反。结果再次失望。

paragraphs.en: One virtue of Julius and Ethel having been dead for nearly three-quarters of a century is that their sons will never have to confront them about what they now know; one difficulty is that they will never get to. This impossibility seems to act as a kind of buffer. They can shake their heads at their father’s actions and tell themselves that their mother did the only thing she could, while reserving their deepest outrage for the one actor they still have the power to wrangle with: the United States government.
paragraphs.zh: 朱利叶斯和伊特尔去世近四分之三个世纪的一个好处，是他们的儿子们永远不必面对父母，去质问如今他们已知晓的事情；一个困难是，他们也永远不能做到。这种“无法面对面对峙”的局面似乎起到了一种缓冲作用。他们可以摇头批评父亲的行为，并告诉自己母亲已经做了她所能做的唯一选择，同时把最深的愤怒留给那个他们仍然有机会角力的对象：美国政府。

paragraphs.en: On several occasions over the course of my reporting, Michael brought up the petition that he and Robby had tried to deliver to Obama in 2016. Still eager for a reply, Michael sent a letter to the Obama Foundation in Chicago in the spring of 2025, and then another—by certified mail—to the foundation’s office in Washington, D.C. He never heard back.
paragraphs.zh: 在我持续采访的过程中，迈克尔多次提到他和罗比曾在2016年试图向奥巴马递交的一份请愿书。由于仍然期待回复，迈克尔在2025年春天给位于芝加哥的奥巴马基金会写了一封信，随后又通过挂号信寄了一封到华盛顿特区的基金会办公室。他始终没有收到任何回音。

paragraphs.en: Recently, Michael told me that he had given up on trying to elicit a response from the former president. “I thought it was a bigger deal than other people did,” he admitted. Robby, for his part, told me he preferred to look forward, not back. He is 79 now; his brother is 83.
paragraphs.zh: 最近，迈克尔告诉我，他已经不再试图从这位前总统那里得到回应。“我觉得这件事比很多人认为的更严重，”他坦白说。罗比则对我说，他更愿意向前看，而不是回头看。他现在79岁；他哥哥83岁。

paragraphs.en: But Robby didn’t mean that he was ready to stop thinking about his parents’ deaths or fighting for his mother’s exoneration—only that he didn’t want to waste any more time on old strategies that hadn’t panned out. The brothers are working with Representative Jim McGovern, Robby’s congressman, who last year made a floor speech in the House about Ethel and has advocated for her exoneration. They’d love to see a major Hollywood movie about their mother, they said, to reignite public interest in the case.
paragraphs.zh: 但罗比并不意味着他已经准备好停止思考父母去世的事情或停止为母亲平反——他只是不愿再把时间浪费在那些没有结果的旧策略上。兄弟俩正在与罗比的众议员吉姆·麦戈文合作。麦戈文去年在众议院就伊瑟尔发表过一次演讲，并一直为她的平反奔走。正如他们所说，他们很希望拍一部关于母亲的好莱坞大型电影，以重新点燃公众对这一案件的关注。

paragraphs.en: Listening to them, I thought about the train tracks Michael had drawn as a kid, endlessly looping back on themselves. Where, if anywhere, might these latest efforts lead? In some ways, the brothers have gotten much further in their quest than they ever anticipated they would; their deep satisfaction at the 2024 release of the Gardner memo came not just from its contents, but also from the somewhat improbable fact that they lived to see it, after 50 years of requests and lawsuits and petitions. It had given them a sense of forward motion, and a measure of vindication late in their lives. They knew far more than they once had. How could they stop now?
paragraphs.zh: 听着他们讲话，我想起了迈克尔小时候画过的那些火车轨道——轨道不停地一圈圈回到自己身上。那些最新的努力究竟会把他们带向哪里，或者说会不会有什么结果？从某种意义上说，兄弟俩在这场追寻中走得比他们想象的还要远；他们对2024年《加德纳备忘录》发布时的深深满足，不仅来自文件内容，也来自一个更不寻常的事实——在持续50年请求、诉讼和请愿之后，他们活着看到了这一刻。它让他们在晚年重新感到一种向前推进的力量，也带来了一定程度的昭雪。如今他们比过去知道得多得多。如今他们又怎能停下？

paragraphs.en: This article appears in the August 2026 print edition with the headline “The Rosenberg Boys.”
paragraphs.zh: 这篇文章以“罗森堡兄弟”为标题，刊登在2026年8月的纸质版中。
